title: Proxy Advisory Firms and Corporate Shareholder Engagement
abstract: We study how Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) affect firms’ engagement with shareholders. Our analyses exploit a quasi-natural experiment using say-on-pay voting outcomes near a threshold that triggers ISS to review engagement activities. Firms receiving ISS treatment exhibit swift and substantive increases in extensive and intensive margins of engagement, especially when their boards have higher agency conflicts and directors are more likely to lose voting support from ISS. Increases in engagement persist over time, and shareholders appear to value the increased engagement. Collectively, we shed light on an unexplored channel through which ISS positively influences firms’ governance and information environments.

article.1 Institutional Background and Hypothese:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Holger Mueller Holger Mueller Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Proxy advisors (PAs) conduct research and provide recommendations to investors that significantly affect voting outcomes (Malenko and Shen 2016). Despite their pivotal role in shaping the information flows that facilitate shareholder voting decisions, the impact of PAs on firm governance and information quality is a topic of debate. Some research suggests that higher-quality PA recommendations can be valuable (Malenko and Malenko 2019;Albuquerque, Carter, and Gallani 2020), while others point to their biased nature, role in standardization of pay plans, reductions in independent information acquisition, and decreases in overall firm value (Larcker, McCall, and Ormazabal 2015;Cabezon 2024;Malenko, Malenko, and Spatt 2024). In line with these concerns, market participants, legislators, and regulators have recently scrutinized the influence of ISS—the largest PA firm—on firms’ governance policies and voting outcomes (Li 2018;SEC 2020;U.S. Congress 2023).

In this paper, we propose an unexplored channel by which ISS likely influences firms’ governance and information environments:shareholder engagement. ISS describes engagement as direct discussions between firms and investors on issues such as executive compensation and corporate governance.1If firms receive what ISS deems to be a low say-on-pay (SOP) vote, it advises managers to meet with shareholders to better understand their concerns. If managers do not demonstrate a “robust engagement response,” ISS will recommend voting against the firm’s next SOP vote and potentially members of the board.

We posit that ISS can affect corporate governance by encouraging firms to engage with their investors. Such engagement can promote a two-way flow of information, enabling managers and directors to better understand investors’ nuanced views than they would through the coarse one-way signal of voting outcomes. If true, then rather than simply imposing its own standardized guidelines, ISS facilitates richer information exchanges between firms and shareholders and elevates shareholders’ influence on corporate governance and firm policies.

Because public companies are not legally obligated to follow the policies of PAs, it is unclear whether we will find evidence of ISS-induced engagement. However, institutional theory argues that private decentralized organizations like PAs can influence managerial behavior through “economic sanctions,” such as influential voting recommendations (Ingram and Silverman 2002;Delmas and Toffel 2008). Moreover, surveys of corporate directors and institutional investors suggest that PAs play an important role in facilitating firm and investor interactions (McCahery, Sautner, and Starks 2016;Hayne and Vance 2019).

To study shareholder engagement, we develop novel metrics based on firms’ voluntary disclosures in proxy statements, where ISS encourages firms to describe their response to a low vote. These metrics reflect the presence (ie, extensive margins) and intensity (ie, intensive margins) of discussions using a count of engagement keywords, and whether shareholder discussions reference the previous SOP vote. Additionally, we gauge whether firms summarize shareholder concerns and the board’s response in tables, and the proportion of shareholders contacted or spoken with. These measures provide a wide assessment of the breadth and depth of a firm’s engagement practices.

We begin our analysis by studying the time trends and determinants of engagement across more than 4,000 firms and over 20,000 SOP votes in the ISS database from 2011 to 2019. In addition to understanding whether PAs, including ISS, play a role in influencing engagement over time, this exploration begins to address the literature gap on the drivers of engagement, which likely stems from the difficulty of observing private conversations. While just 5.5% of firms report shareholder engagement in 2011, it rises to 36.3% by 2019, with an average of 24% during our sample period. This trend coincides with increasing calls for engagement from PAs, shareholders, and regulators.2

In our regressions of the determinants of engagement, we find that firm size, leverage, institutional ownership, analyst coverage, and CEO pay positively correlate with the extensive and intensive margins of engagement. Profitability, market-to-book values, and also certain governance characteristics (classified boards, dual class shares, and compensation committee tenure) are negatively related to engagement. Importantly, we find that SOP voting dissent as well as ISS issuing an “against” recommendation for an SOP proposal both precede higher levels of engagement. Given the effect of ISS voting recommendations, these findings suggest that ISS could strongly influence firms’ shareholder engagement policies.

To determine whether ISS’s influence on engagement is causal, we exploit ISS’s policy mandating a specific response from firms receiving less than 70% SOP voting support, using this threshold as a discontinuity for identification purposes. Should a firm’s SOP vote fall below 70%, ISS formally scrutinizes its disclosed shareholder engagement to decide on its voting guidance before the next annual meeting. ISS’s policies specify what disclosures are needed for firms to show a robust engagement response to a low vote, which dictates ISS’s voting recommendations and its enhanced scrutiny. Failure to show sufficient response could lead ISS to advise against the SOP vote, compensation committee members, or even the entire board.

Our identifying assumption is that firms cannot precisely manipulate the SOP votes around the 70% threshold. This assumption is supported by a smooth distribution at the ISS cutoff, confirmed through graphical analyses of SOP plots and a formal robust marginal density test (McCrary 2008). We also examine prior engagement, SOP voting, firm characteristics, CEO pay, and governance before treatment and find sparse statistical differences in these properties between firms just below 70% SOP support (treatment) and those just above (control). These results suggest treatment around this threshold is as good as random, enabling us to attribute subsequent changes in engagement to ISS’s influence.

Utilizing linear regression, difference-in-differences (d-i-d), and regression discontinuity (RD) methodologies, we find that ISS treatment leads to a significant increase in the extensive and intensive margins of shareholder engagement in the year after a low SOP vote. The linear regression tests reveal ISS-treated firms are 31% more likely to disclose engagement than control firms. Additionally, treated firms show greater breadth of engagement by contacting and speaking with investors owning a larger number of shares. They also elaborate more on their engagement efforts by offering more comprehensive details in their proxy statements.

These results support the theory that private organizations like ISS can motivate firms to strengthen shareholder engagement using the threat of negative voting recommendations. To further substantiate the causal impact of ISS on engagement, we conduct several cross-sectional analyses. If ISS plays an important role in enhancing engagement and provides what market participants view as a credible threat, we anticipate a noticeably larger engagement response by directors that are at greater risk of economic sanctions. Consistent with this notion, we observe larger engagement increases among treated firms with lower prior voting support for directors as a whole and compensation committee members in particular.

To further test the credibility of ISS’s threat, we designate firms as more or less responsive to the SOP vote based on whether they disclose engagement efforts in their subsequent proxy. We find that less responsive treated firms have a greater likelihood of receiving an “against” recommendation by ISS for the entire board, and especially members of the compensation committee. These findings help validate ISS’s threat against directors who do not demonstrate a robust response, thereby supporting the mechanism of ISS shaping firms’ engagement decisions through economic sanctions, which increases the confidence in our causal inferences.

A natural question arises regarding the sustainability of engagement efforts after treatment. On the one hand, firms could reduce their engagement activities after satisfying ISS’s requirements. This finding would be intuitive as elevated ISS oversight ends after demonstrating a robust engagement response, and failure to engage does not result in SEC penalties since SOP votes are nonbinding. On the other hand, treated firms might demonstrate a commitment to engagement to avoid further negative attention from ISS. Moreover, engagement likely involves a one-time cost for establishing engagement protocols and identifying shareholders to engage with. Firms could reap the benefits of sustained engagement with low marginal costs beyond the first year, potentially leading to greater goodwill among shareholders, improved efficiency of future engagement efforts, and a convergence of beliefs between management and shareholders (Kakhbod et al. 2023). Consistent with the latter thesis, we find that ISS-treated firms show enduring increases in shareholder engagement in the years after, but not before, the low SOP vote.

Next, using an event study around the SOP vote disclosure, we investigate whether ISS-facilitated engagement benefits shareholders. Our findings reveal a positive stock price response when firms both fall just below the 70% treatment threshold and for which ISS had issued an “against” SOP vote recommendation. This stock price response is economically meaningful, with positive abnormal returns between 2% and 3%. These findings suggest that shareholders anticipate that the benefits of strengthened engagement will accrue to investors when the threat of ISS monitoring and economic sanctions is strongest.

Given that markets respond favorably to anticipated engagement, and firms encouraged by ISS to start engaging continue to do so, our final set of tests explore why treated firms did not engage before ISS intervention. In particular, we examine whether the presence of agency conflicts on the board of directors plays a role in engagement policies. We anticipate and find that boards with properties that are typically associated with higher agency conflicts, such as long-tenured compensation committee members and classified boards, increase engagement more after ISS treatment. This finding suggests that ISS can play a substitute governance role for such firms.

Our study faces some limitations. A primary concern relates to our reliance on voluntary disclosures for measuring shareholder engagement, which may not reflect the actual level of engagement. It is comforting, however, that the legal liability of firms for their proxy disclosures to the SEC reduces the likelihood of false or misleading reports.3Moreover, while not mandated by the SEC, firms have incentives to disclose engagement activities that are perceived by shareholders and PAs as a gauge of board responsiveness. Another important caveat is that while increased engagement facilitated by ISS appears to be beneficial for firms based on the market response, our study is not designed to determine the optimal level of engagement for the firm and its investors.

Our study contributes to several strands of research. Our primary contribution is to the literature on the economic role of PAs (e.g.,Alexander et al. 2010;Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch 2013;Malenko and Shen 2016;Shu 2024), particularity ISS, in shaping corporate engagement policies. We show that ISS’s policies promote two-way communication between firms and shareholders after low SOP votes. This greater and sustained engagement likely allows for a more effective exchange of shareholder concerns and managerial strategies beyond simple one-way coarse communication through voting. Our event study results suggest that shareholders value ISS’s role in encouraging this dialogue.

Our results on ISS’s economic sanctions on directors, and their engagement policies in response, adds to the literature on labor market consequences for directors (e.g.,Fama and Jensen 1983;Srinivasan 2005;Fich and Shivdasani 2007;Del Guercio, Seery, and Woidtke 2008). We show that ISS can incentivize directors to promote engagement policies to preserve their board position and reputation as effective monitors.

Our findings also offer novel insights into ISS’s impact on capital markets, challenging the view that PAs simply impose one-size-fits-all pay policies on firms (Larcker, McCall, and Ormazabal 2015;Jochem, Ormazabal, and Rajamani 2021;Cabezon 2024). In our setting, ISS encourages firms to engage with investors and consider their perspectives based on the unique circumstances of each firm and its investor base. Thus, our results expand on our understanding of how ISS can serve an important governance role (Malenko and Malenko 2019;Albuquerque, Carter, and Gallani 2020) and complement survey evidence that PAs can improve shareholder engagement activities (McCahery, Sautner, and Starks 2016;Hayne and Vance 2019).

These results could also inform policy debates, potentially alleviating concerns among regulators and lawmakers about ISS’s significant market power and its effects on corporate governance quality (Financial Economists Roundtable 2022;U.S. Congress 2023). Although our findings cannot answer whether ISS creates or destroys value on net, we show that by encouraging engagement, ISS enables investor preferences to be heard and potentially incorporated in firm policies. In this way, ISS could facilitate better governance decisions for individual firms. Thus, our research could provide valuable insights for contemporaneous discussions on regulating the scope of ISS’s voting advisory services (e.g.,Li 2018).

Finally, our paper provides an important contribution to the nascent strand of literature examining engagement between firms and their investors. We introduce a novel disclosure-based approach to provide the first measures of different dimensions of shareholder engagement by firms and document various factors that determine engagement decisions. We complement the findings byKakhbod et al. (2023), who provide a theory of engagement that, when shareholders and management have different beliefs about corporate policies, the efficacy of shareholder engagement increases when other shareholders also engage. We extend this literature by showing that firms under elevated ISS scrutiny engage more with their investors by strengthening the breadth and depth of engagement.
1.1 Background information and conceptual framework

PAs play a pivotal role in corporate governance by providing institutional investors with paid guidance on proxy voting matters, based on comprehensive research, analysis, and investor consultations (GAO 2016). They synthesize a large amount of information to make informed recommendations, which can help institutional investors make efficient voting decisions across their diverse portfolios (Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch 2013;Malenko and Malenko 2019).

The demand for PA services has increased in recent years due to shareholders’ needs for informed voting decisions, increased institutional ownership, and regulatory mandates like SOP. The PA advisory market is dominated by ISS and Glass Lewis (GL), which together control more than 90% of the U.S. market (Copland, Larcker, and Tayan 2018;Shu 2024). Research confirms their sizeable influence on voting outcomes (Choi, Fisch, and Kahan 2009;Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch 2013;Larcker, McCall, and Ormazabal 2015;Malenko and Shen 2016). Specifically, an ISS “against” recommendation for a firm’s SOP can decrease shareholder support by 25% (Malenko and Shen 2016), while a negative GL recommendation can lead to a 13% decline (Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch 2013).4

The influence of PAs, however, has drawn regulatory scrutiny and sparked debate over their role in governance, information flows, and firm value (Alexander et al. 2010;Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch 2013;Malenko and Shen 2016;Larcker, McCall, and Ormazabal 2013,2015;Shu 2024). While some studies highlight the negative effects, including biased advice, generic policies, crowding out information acquisition, and diminished shareholder wealth (e.g.,Iliev and Lowry 2015;Larcker, McCall, and Ormazabal 2015;Li 2018;Malenko and Malenko 2019;Levit and Tsoy 2022;Cabezon 2024;Malenko, Malenko, and Spatt 2024), others suggest high-quality PA recommendations can be beneficial (Malenko and Malenko 2019), particularly during less busy times when more resources are available (Albuquerque, Carter, and Gallani 2020).

Panel A ofFigure 1depicts the assumed traditional information flow under PA voting guidance. The conventional framework for PA influence on firm policies is by selling voting recommendations and advice to institutional investors, helping them make informed voting decisions on proxy ballot items. However, this model suggests a simplistic and potentially incomplete information exchange between shareholders and firms. Additionally, challenges in aggregating information and preferences could curtail the effectiveness of this exchange (Levit, Malenko, and Maug 2024).

We propose shareholder engagement as an alternative mechanism through which PAs can affect the flow of information between firms and investors. Such engagement could promote continuous, two-way communication, enabling firms to better understand shareholder preferences for corporate strategies, beyond simple one-way voting signals. Proactive engagement with shareholders before voting could allow firms to incorporate feedback into decision-making and potentially modify the subsequent voting proposal to sway voting outcomes (e.g., adjust executive compensation for SOP votes).5

Our conceptual model suggests that PAs could enhance the integration of detailed shareholder feedback into corporate policies, especially following a low SOP vote. This perspective predicts that PAs significantly affect governance and the dynamics of information flows in capital markets, thereby challenging traditional views. Panel B ofFigure 1illustrates our conceptual approach to understanding engagement-driven information exchange under PA guidance.6We explore this theory by examining the engagement policies of ISS, the largest PA firm.

1.2 ISS policy on shareholder engagement

ISS’s policies state that, if support for its SOP proposal falls below 70% of votes cast, it will evaluate a firm’s responsiveness through engagement. Though ISS does not directly facilitate discussions between firms and shareholders, it requires managers and/or directors to proactively reach out to shareholders and then clearly disclose both quantitative and qualitative aspects about this engagement and subsequent actions taken in response to these concerns in the next annual proxy statement.7

ISS also expects firms to disclose the number and share percentage of shareholders they engage with, specific shareholder concerns that led to SOP dissent, concrete actions taken to address these concerns, or explain any reasons for not acting. Additionally, the firm should disclose its plans to prevent similar issues from occurring in the future.

ISS stipulates that if a firm demonstrates a “sufficiently robust response” to shareholder concerns in its proxy statement following a low vote, it will not require future engagement disclosures, provided that future SOP votes achieve at least 70% support. Failure to adequately respond to a low vote will lead ISS to advise against the next SOP proposal and the incumbent compensation committee members. A “moderate” response will result in ISS recommending against only the next SOP proposal, sparing compensation committee members from economic sanctions. Repeated instances of inadequate responses will prompt ISS to recommend voting against the entire board due to systemic issues with board stewardship and oversight.8

1.3 Hypotheses development: Level and persistence of shareholder engagement

Although ISS lacks legal authority to mandate firms’ engagement practices, our study on its influence over corporate behavior draws from the tenets of institutional theory within organizational studies. This theory examines how institutions, including private decentralized entities like ISS, can shape the actions of organizations (Ingram and Clay 2000;Ingram and Silverman 2002;Delmas and Toffel 2008). Despite lacking formal enforcement capabilities, entities like ISS can still affect managerial practices through the potential for economic penalties for failing to comply with its guidelines (Ellickson 1991;Ingram and Silverman 2002;Terlaak 2007).

ISS offers advisory services to institutional investors by informing their votes on shareholder proxy matters in publicly traded companies. While these firms have no legal obligation to follow ISS policies or alter engagement or disclosure practices after receiving less than 70% support in SOP votes, ISS can exert pressure by threatening to recommend against future SOP or director votes for firms that do not demonstrate a robust engagement response in their next proxy filing. Considering ISS’s substantial influence on voting outcomes (Malenko and Shen 2016), we expect the prospect of a negative recommendation will motivate firms to increase shareholder engagement and disclose the related information in the subsequent proxy statement following a low SOP vote. This leads to our first hypothesis:

H1: Firms with SOP voting support slightly below 70% will follow ISS requirements by increasing shareholder engagement in the following year.

ISS’s guidelines recommend voting against future SOP proposals as well as against the reelection of compensation committee members and the entire board if firms fail to adequately address poor outcomes in SOP votes. Prior research has suggested that labor market incentives can motivate directors to perform well in their roles. Underperforming directors are more likely to face career consequences, including losing current directorships and not obtaining new ones (e.g.,Fama and Jensen 1983;Srinivasan 2005;Fich and Shivdasani 2007;Del Guercio, Seery, and Woidtke 2008;Cai, Garner, and Walkling 2009). Given the potential career impact of ISS’s economic sanctions, it is plausible that boards at greater risk of such penalties would be more proactive in engaging with shareholders and ensuring these efforts are disclosed in their subsequent proxy statements. To examine these arguments, we propose the following hypotheses.

First, we predict that firms with compensation committee and board members who have already experienced low votes will be more motivated to enhance their engagement efforts due to concerns over ISS’s economic repercussions. Formally, we state following hypothesis:

H2a: Firms with directors on the compensation committee or the board with low prior SOP voting support will have higher shareholder engagement after ISS intervention.

Second, should the threat by ISS to impose economic sanctions on unresponsive firms be credible, we expect directors of firms that do not demonstrate a satisfactory engagement response following a low SOP vote will be more likely to receive “against” recommendations from ISS for their reelection. This anticipation of an adverse recommendation on their future candidacy could motivate boards to proactively adhere to ISS’s guidelines. As a result, we predict an increase in negative recommendations for firms that fail to meet ISS’s policy expectations. Thus, we articulate the next hypothesis as follows:

H2b: Directors, especially those serving on the compensation committee, of firms subjected to ISS intervention and displaying lower levels of engagement are more likely to face “against” recommendations from ISS for their reelection in the following year.

If a firm demonstrates robust shareholder engagement following a low vote and achieves a vote above 70% in the next year, ISS will no longer subject it to increased scrutiny. However, firms that are relieved of intense scrutiny may choose to continue their shareholder engagement activities in the longer term. Prior to receiving a low SOP vote, firms may have been reluctant to engage with shareholders, believing the costs of engagement to exceed the benefits. These costs could include the initial fixed setup expenses for creating an engagement infrastructure, such as defining engagement protocols for which firm personnel will engage, identifying the shareholders to interact with, developing the necessary technology for communication, and organizing legal guidelines.

After the initial year of ISS-induced engagement, and once the engagement infrastructure is in place, the marginal benefits, such as improved information flow quality, and the development of goodwill and supportive shareholder relationships (Kakhbod et al. 2023), may outweigh the marginal costs of maintaining this engagement infrastructure. Further, the initial interactions might more closely align the perspectives of management and shareholders (Crawford and Sobel 1982), improving the effectiveness of future engagements, which are more likely to be initiated by management on a voluntary basis. Other factors for sustaining engagement could be that firms might also aim to maintain their shareholder support above the 70% threshold to avoid negative scrutiny from ISS or shareholders in the future. Additionally, directors may want to maintain their reputation as effective monitors and could be motivated to show their commitment to engagement and transparency to bolster their reelection prospects. With these factors in mind, we propose the following hypothesis:

H3: ISS-induced increases in shareholder engagement following SOP voting support just below 70% will not be short-lived.

If the evidence supports H3, it would be consistent with findings from surveys conducted byHayne and Vance (2019). Their research finds that both directors and compensation committees believe that PAs can enhance firm communication by “providing an impetus for firms to engage with institutional investors.”

Several forces, however, could obscure our ability to find evidence supporting H1, H2, or H3. First, since advisory votes on executive compensation are nonbinding, firms do not face SEC penalties for low approval rates of their executive pay policies. Second, firms with just over 70% approval may react in a similar manner to those with slightly less than 70% approval, as both are within the lowest quintile for support of their compensation practices.9Third, firms slightly above the 70% threshold might anticipate falling below the threshold in future SOP votes and could thus increase engagement with shareholders to avoid negative attention from ISS. Fourth, receiving slightly less than 70% approval still has the majority of shareholders backing its executive pay practices, potentially leading firms to not increase their engagement efforts unless SOP support falls below 50%. Lastly, the perceived costs of increasing engagement might deter managers and/or directors from increasing their engagement levels.

It is also possible that evidence might support H1 and H2, but not H3. This scenario could take place if firms increase their interactions with shareholders following a poor SOP vote outcome merely to align with ISS guidelines. After meeting ISS’s guidelines for a robust engagement response, the firm might consider the compensation issue resolved and return to its previous level of shareholder engagement.

1.4 Hypotheses development: Shareholder reactions to ISS treatment

We explore whether ISS-induced engagement is beneficial through an event study centered on the announcement of SOP results. Forced intervention by ISS may result in wasteful use of managerial time and resources, along with potentially value-diminishing policy adjustments (Larcker, McCall, and Ormazabal 2015). Such a scenario could result in negative announcement returns for treated firms. It is also possible that firms meet with shareholders, but investors expect any changes to compensation or governance policies to be immaterial. In this scenario, we might not observe an announcement return that is significantly different from zero. Another possible outcome is that shareholders expect ISS’s policies will foster greater communication between managers and shareholders that generate value for the firm. In this case, we might observe positive and significant returns for treated firms. Given the competing forces, we state the following hypothesis in the null form:

H4a:Treated firms will not experience significant returns around the SOP vote announcement.

It is possible that a significant shareholder reaction might only occur when the looming threat of ISS enforcement is the strongest. In this scenario, we could observe positive stock returns when a firm is both treatedandpreviously received an “against” SOP recommendation from ISS, indicating a heightened risk of economic sanctions against board members. Thus, as an extension, we test this hypothesis:

H4b:Treated firms will experience positive returns when they have already received “against” recommendations from ISS.

Evidence supporting H1 through H4 would strengthen our argument that ISS plays a crucial role in capital markets by promoting better shareholder engagement, enabling ongoing and bidirectional information exchange between firms and investors. Such findings could also attenuate concerns that ISS’s policies lead to a one-size-fits-all approach to governance and compensation. Instead, evidence in support of H1 to H4 would be consistent with the notion that ISS motivates managers and directors to interact with shareholders and address diverse issues unique to each firm.
article.2 Sample and Engagement Measures:
2.1 Sample selection

We construct our sample by downloading all SOP voting outcomes from the ISS Voting Analytics database over calendar years 2011 to 2019. From this sample, 20,610 firm-years for 4,075 unique firms are matched with proxy statement information on the SEC’s website. We use this sample for two types of analysis. For the first analysis, aimed at identifying engagement determinants, we include all 20,610 firm years in which a firm discloses SOP voting results.

For the second analysis, we focus on a subsample of 426 firms that, for the first time during our sample period, disclose an SOP vote within the narrow ±2.5% range around the 70% support level.10We defineSOP voting supportfollowing the ISS definition of votes for the SOP proposal divided by the votes for and against SOP. Abstention votes are not included in the ISS definition; thus, we do not include them in our definition of voting support. The selection criteria for the second analysis allows for clean testing of our hypotheses. To ensure the completeness of our sample, we cross-verify our data set against a file provided directly by ISS that lists all firms with SOP votes under 70%.

We merge these data with the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) and Compustat databases and exclude observations with missing stock price or accounting information used as controls. Our sample for this second analysis contains 209 firms with votes just below the 70% threshold (treated firms) and 217 firms just above (control firms). The sample is approximately equally distributed across our sample period. We defineISS treatmentas an indicator variable that equals one for firms with SOP voting support between 67.50% and 69.99%, and zero for those with support between 70.00% and 72.50%.Appendix Aprovides a timeline of ISS treatment and subsequent changes in shareholder engagement, pay disclosure, and the event study, all of which we will define below.

2.2 Measures of shareholder engagement

2.2.1 Extensive and intensive margins of engagement

Our shareholder engagement analysis relies primarily on textual analysis of proxy statements, where ISS encourages firms to voluntarily disclose engagement activities so they can monitor the response. We provide examples of engagement disclosure inAppendix B. To construct these measures, we develop a Python script to extract text from the proxy statement. Our script then flags engagement keywords (engage, feedback, conversation) and their variants within a 100-character range of shareholder keywords (shareholder, stockholder) and their variants. For the subsample of firms in the ±2.5% range around the 70% SOP voting support, we hand check the proxy statements for accuracy, but our inferences are unchanged if we rely solely on the script.11

From this keyword identification process, we generate two variables to capture the extensive and intensive margins of shareholder engagement during our 9-year sample period. For our extensive measure,engagement indicatorequals one if the firm mentions any engagement keyword near a shareholder keyword in the proxy statement, and else zero. This measure captures the presence of engagement following the prior shareholder meeting. For our intensive measure,engagement countis the number of engagement keywords near shareholder keywords within the proxy statement. We assume that more engagement discussions in the proxy reflects greater engagement intensity.

Table 1andFigure 2display time trends in shareholder engagement for the full sample. We find that at least one mention of shareholder engagement was disclosed for 5.7% of firms in 2011, rising to 16.0% in 2012, and monotonically increasing to 37.0% in 2019. The intensity of engagement also grows over time, starting at an average of 0.18 engagement words per firm in 2011 and increasing to 1.26 by 2019. During the sample period, 23.4% of firms in the ISS database disclose the presence of shareholder engagement, averaging 0.66 engagement words. When engagement occurs, firms provide an average of 2.62 engagement words in their annual proxy during the sample period (untabulated).

2.2.2 Depth and breadth of engagement

To capture engagement depth, we hand collect two points of information from the proxy statement in the year after the low SOP vote (yeart+ 1). Some firms note that their engagement efforts are a direct response to a low SOP vote in the prior year. Thus, we create the variable,engagement references SOP, which equals one if the proxy statement links the firm’s engagement efforts to the prior SOP vote, and else zero. As shown inAppendix B, firms sometimes provide tables that follow the format of “what we heard” and “our response” to investor concerns that led to voting dissent. A table highlighting shareholder concerns and the firm’s responses could demonstrate that the firm takes engagement seriously, potentially boost investor confidence and reduce information processing costs.12Thus, we create a variable,engagement table, which equals one if the firm includes a summary table of its engagement activity, and otherwise 0.

Some firms highlight the extent of shareholder engagement based on the holdings of investors they contact or speak with. Thus, to assess engagement breadth, we measure (1) the percentage of shares owned by investors contacted after a low SOP vote (shareholders contacted) and(2)the percentage of shares held by investors who agreed to speak with the firm (shareholders spoken with). Both variables offer precise insight into the firm’s outreach efforts and enhance the credibility of our textual-based engagement proxies. Not all firms disclose these data. For those that do not and have anengagement indicatorof zero, we set bothshareholders contactedandshareholders spoken withto zero. If a firm has anengagement indicatorof one but lacks disclosure ofshareholders contactedorshareholders spoken with, we classify these values as missing.Table IA-4 in the Internet Appendixreveals that our engagement measures are highly, but not perfectly, correlated. Thus, measuring various aspects of shareholder engagement helps us capture firms’ actions more comprehensively.

Since the measures of the depth and breadth of engagement must be hand collected, we randomly select 900 proxy statements uniformly across the sample period.Table 1shows an increase in firms referencing the prior year’s SOP vote, increasing from 1% in 2011 to 22% in 2019. However, there was little change in the percentage ofshareholders contactedorshareholders spoken withduring this period. Engaging firms contact and converse with holders of approximately 52% and 45% of shares outstanding, respectively, when these metrics are reported. Engagement tables are rare in the overall sample, with only 1.4% presenting these details. However, as shown inTable 5below, 19% of the firms just below the 70% SOP threshold provide engagement tables, indicating this practice is more common after lower SOP voting support.
article.3 Determinants of Engagement:
3.1 Potential determinants

This section explores factors that might influence shareholder engagement using the full sample of 20,610 firm years. Given that our study introduces novel engagement measures, the goal of this exercise is to lay the groundwork for understanding the dynamics of shareholder engagement and its association with firm attributes. Although our empirical framework cannot exogenously identify all determinants, this analysis offers valuable insights that can help validate our engagement measures and bolster our analysis of proxy advisors’ impact on shareholder engagement. Additionally, it provides novel descriptive evidence on a trend that has grown significantly in capital markets, as highlighted inTable 1andFigure 2.

In choosing the determinants to analyze, we first look to factors identified in prior studies of SOP voting (e.g.,Malenko and Shen 2016). For example, we examine how engagement relates to the firm’s SOP voting outcomes and recommendations from PAs. Using ISS Voting Analytics data, we generate the variable,ISS “against” SOP, which equals one if ISS recommends voting against SOP, and else zero. We followShu (2024)in obtaining GL data for a subsample of 20% of firm years using a Freedom of Information Act request from the North Carolina Retirement System. We generate a variable,GL “against” SOP, which equals one if GL recommends voting against SOP and else zero. We anticipate that firms with negative PA recommendations and higher SOP voting dissent will engage more with investors in the subsequent year.

We expect that firms with more institutional ownership and analyst following will have stronger incentives to engage with shareholders due to higher demand for firm information (Boone and White 2015). We use IBES Academic data and defineln(analysts)as the log-transformed value of one plus the number of analysts providing earnings per share forecasts during the four quarters prior to the annual meeting. Data on institutional ownership are from SEC 13-F filings in the Thomson Reuters Institutional Holdings database. We define totalinstitutional ownership (IO)as the total shares held by these institutions divided by shares outstanding from CRSP. The type of institutional investors (ie, passive versus active) might affect engagement (Kakhbod et al. 2023). Therefore, we separate the proportion of IO into different types of institutional investors (dedicated, transient, orquasi-indexer) based on portfolio turnover, diversification, and investment horizons as described inBushee (1998).

Our determinants analysis also includes other factors that might influence shareholder engagement tendencies. We expect that firm characteristics, such assize, market-to-book, return on assets, leverage, sales growth, abnormal returns, stock return volatility, andoperating loss, might affect a firm’s propensity to engage with shareholders about components of executive compensation. We merge these measures using data from CRSP and Compustat and define all variables inAppendix C.

The SEC requires most firms to provide a section in their annual proxy statement called the Compensation Disclosure & Analysis (CD&A), that details information about executive pay, including the goals, design, and reasoning behind the pay program, and the use of peer benchmarking. The SEC allows small firms that meet the definition of a Smaller Reporting Company (SRC) to forego detailed disclosures about executive pay, but they must still hold an SOP vote. We label these firms asCD&A exemptusing an indicator variable if it had a public float less than $75 million before September 10, 2018, or less than $250 million after that date (SEC 2018). We manually check annual SEC Form 10-Ks and identify 33 sample firms as SRCs. The expected impact of not providing a CD&A on engagement is unclear. While the absence of a CD&A could increase engagement efforts to clarify compensation policies—asBalsam et al. (2016)link CD&A prominence to SOP voting results—it might also result in reduced engagement due to the exemption from CD&A requirements.

Firms with higher CEO pay might engage more with shareholders to explain the components of executive pay. We partition pay levels intoCEO equity payandCEO nonequity paybecause shareholders might be more concerned about how CEOs are compensated, rather than the overall level of pay.

Firms with stronger board oversight and governance of executive pay may engage more with shareholders. To proxy for oversight and governance, we follow studies (e.g.,Knyazeva, Knyazeva, and Masulis 2013) in measuringboard independence, classified boards, anddual class sharesfor firms in the ISS Governance database. Since U.S. exchanges require almost all firms to have a fully independent compensation committee, we measure pay governance using the averagecompensation committee tenure, which prior work links to higher CEO pay (Vafeas 2003). Finally, we assess whetherCEO turnover, which is known to influence pay changes (Denis, Jochem, and Rajamani 2020), alters a firm’s engagement policies in the prior year.

3.2 Determinants results

We examine the determinants of shareholder engagement via two types of analyses. The first is a univariate analysis of the relation between the extensive margins of engagement and each individual firm or board characteristic. The purpose of this exercise is to see how each factor relates to engagement in isolation. In our second analysis, we examine both the extensive and intensive margins of engagement in a multivariate setting to identify the potential overall drivers of engagement.

3.2.1 Univariate analysis of engagement

We examine correlations between theengagement indicatorand characteristics of 20,610 firm years during 2011 to 2019. We sort the sample into quintiles based on yearly firm-level characteristics in fiscal yeart. We then compute the proportion of firms engaging with shareholders within each quintile in yeart+ 1, which we present in columns 1 to 5 ofTable 2. For each variable, we compare the highest and lowest quintile (Q5 minus Q1) in column 6 and test whether this difference is statistically significant using two-tailedt-tests, with the associatedp-value reported in column 7. Since we do not have full data on all measures for all sample years, we report the percent coverage of each variable in column 8.

The results show that engagement varies substantially within the quintiles of each firm characteristic. For example, 21.5% of firms in the lowest quintile of SOP voting dissent engaged with investors while 48.5% of those in the highest quintile engaged with shareholders. The difference of 27 percentage points is significantly different from zero at the 1% level.

Firms with recommendations to vote against SOP by ISS or GL have a significantly higher propensity to engage. For these firms, the extent of engagement is 23.8- and 21.1-percentage-points higher, respectively. These tests provide initial evidence that PA recommendations could influence engagement.13

The rest of panel A shows that engagement varies across firm characteristics. Firms that are larger (size), more profitable (return-on-assets), have lessstock return volatility, more analyst coverage, and greaterleveragetend to engage more and this relation is mostly monotonic across the quintiles. Firms with higher growth opportunities (market-to-book) andabnormal returnsengage more, but the relation is not increasing across all quintiles. Firms withCEO turnover, anoperating loss, and those that are notCD&A exempthave higher engagement, however, there is no difference among the highest and lowest quintiles ofsales growth.

Firms with more overallinstitutional ownershiptend to have a higher propensity to engage. This relation appears to be monotonic across the quintiles. Because we observe similar variation in engagement among the different types of institutional ownership (ie, dedicated, transient, and quasi-indexer), we include overall institutional ownership as a control in our multivariate analysis.

Firms with higher CEO pay (equity and nonequity) also tend to engage more with investors. The difference in the engagement propensity between the highest and lowest quintile for CEO equity pay and CEO nonequity pay is 20.1 and 13.8 percentage points, respectively. Engagement is lower for firms with a lower proportion of independent directors, dual class shares, classified boards, and where members of the compensation committee have longer tenure.

3.2.2 Multivariate analysis of engagement

Next, we explore the determinants of shareholder engagement using multivariate regressions. For these tests we estimate the following equations: y i t + 1 = α + β SOP voting dissen t i t + X i t + φ j + μ t + ε i t , (1a) y i t + 1 = α + β ISS ‘ ‘ against ″ S O P i t + X i t + φ j + μ t + ε i t , (1b) where the dependent variable y i t + 1 includes the two primary measures of engagement ( engagement indicator and engagement count ). The coefficients of interest are β , which captures how engagement relates to SOP voting ( Equation (1a) ) and the SOP voting recommendation of ISS ( Equation (1b) ) in the prior year. To avoid multicollinearity, we do not include both SOP voting dissent and ISS “against” SOP in the same regression since ISS “against” recommendations are strongly correlated with the SOP voting dissent (69%) and are causally linked to SOP voting outcomes ( Malenko and Shen 2016 ).

Both equations include fixed effects (FEs) for the firm’s industry (⁠φj⁠) using one-digit Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) codes, which controls for time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity across industries, and fiscal year FEs (μt) to control for time trends that affect all firms. We also estimateEquations (1a)and(1b)by replacing industry FEs with firm FEs. Standard errors are double clustered at the year and industry levels for regressions with industry FEs, and at the year and firm levels for those with firm FEs.

Xitis a vector of firm-level covariates. These include the natural logarithm ofCEO equityandnonequity pay, board independence, compensation committee tenure, classified board, size, market-to-book, return-on-assets, leverage, sales growth, abnormal returns, stock return volatility, operating loss, CD&A exempt, ln(analysts), institutional ownership, andCEO turnover. We measure accounting variables in the same fiscal year as reported CEO pay.

Table 3reports the results. Columns 1 through 4 show the tests of theengagement indicator, which is estimated using ordinary least squares (OLS) regressions. We use a linear probability model rather than a probit or logit to avoid bias from FEs (Greene 2004). Columns 5 through 8 report the determinants ofengagement count, which is estimated using a Poisson regression (Cohn, Liu, and Wardlaw 2022).

These relations are similar when industry FEs are replaced with firm FEs in columns 3 and 4 for theengagement indicatorand columns 7 and 8 forengagement count.15

Across many specifications,size, leverage, and CEO pay tend to be positively related to engagement. Similarly, there is evidence thatanalyst following, institutional ownership, andabnormal returnsare positively related to engagement at the 5% level in some regressions. Conversely, firms withclassified boards, dual classshares, highermarket-to-book, and higher profit (return-on-assets) tend to disclose less engagement.Compensation committee tenureis negatively related to engagement with industry FEs, but positively related to engagement count with firm FEs. Thus, in the cross section, higher compensation committee tenure appears to weaken monitoring. But, within a firm, once a compensation committee begins engaging, it likely continues to do so.Board independence, stock return volatility, sales growth, operating losses, CD&A exemption, andCEO turnoverlack or have significance below the 5% level in all regressions. We exclude them as controls in our regressions of engagement in Section 5 to avoid overspecification issues (Whited et al. 2022). The results are not sensitive to this choice.
article.4 ISS and Shareholder Engagement:
The results inTables 2and3suggest a strong relation between ISS recommendations, SOP voting, and shareholder engagement. In this section, we use an identification strategy to determine whether ISS’s policiescauseincreases in engagement. We first explain and validate the identification strategy before using regression techniques to show the causal link between ISS and engagement.

4.1 Identification strategy

ISS considers firms with less than 70% SOP support as problematic and reviews their responsiveness to shareholder concerns. Firms with SOP voting support just below 70% receive ISS treatment, while those above do not. Thus, our identifying assumption is that whether a firm receives ISS treatment is locally random around this threshold.

We consider whether the 70% threshold is specific to ISS or is also a significant threshold for other key constituents. GL, the second-largest proxy advisory firm in the US, considers SOP results below 75% support from 2011 to 2017 and below 80% from 2018 onward as a trigger in its policies for encouraging shareholder engagement.16Thus, GL’s treatment encompasses our treated and control firms near ISS’s 70% cutoff and should not drive our results.

Large institutional shareholders might also deem 70% to be an important threshold. To shed light on this question, we take two approaches. First, we examine survey results from ISS from 2011, when they established their shareholder engagement policy. As shown inFigure 3, ISS asked both investors and firms at what SOP level should the board explicitly respond to issues related to pay practices. The possible choices were 50%, 60%, 70%, 80%, and 90%.

Fig. 3 ISS survey on shareholder engagement This figure charts investor and firm responses to a say-on-pay (SOP) survey conducted by Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) in 2011. The survey asked investors and firms, “At what level of opposition on a say-on-pay proposal should there be an explicit response from the board regarding improvements on pay practices?” We tabulate responses across five thresholds on the x -axis and graph the percentage of investors and firms responding with support for each SOP voting threshold on the y -axis. See ISS (2011) . Open in new tab Download slide

As the graph inFigure 3depicts, the most common response from investors was 80%, while firms most frequently selected 50%. Thus, the figure depicts the divergence in investors’ and firms’ views and clarifies that the 70% threshold selected by ISS lacked unanimous support between market participants.

Second, previous research has shown that large investors (e.g., BlackRock) engage with portfolio firms on governance issues (Pawliczek, Skinner, and Wellman 2021). To help rule out that these investors’ engagement policies drive our results, we show inTable IA-6 of the Internet Appendixthat our engagement results are not driven by ownership of the “Big 3” asset managers (ie, BlackRock, State Street, and Vanguard). We also examine the stated policies of Blackrock, State Street, and Vanguard and find no language requiring portfolio firms to engage after a certain level of SOP dissent.

Next, we examine SOP voting outcomes for the full sample of 20,610 firm years to test whether receiving ISS treatment is random near the 70% threshold.Figure 4plots the distribution ofSOP voting supportfor firms with support between 50% and 90% (panel A) and 67.5% to 72.5% (panel B). The distribution shows no visual clustering around the 70% threshold in either panel, providing initial evidence that treatment is locally random.

Fig. 4 Distribution of say-on-pay voting support This figure presents a histogram of the percentage of shareholders approving say-on-pay (SOP) using the ISS definition of SOP voting support . Panel A presents the density of firms with voting support between 50% and 90%, while panel B shows the density of firms with voting support between 67.5% and 72.5%. Open in new tab Download slide

We then conduct a formal test to check for manipulation of the assignment variable around the 70% cutoff (McCrary 2008). Specifically, we use the local polynomial density estimation ofCattaneo, Jansson, and Ma (2020)(hereinafter “CJM”), who develop a robust nonparametric density estimator to test for manipulation in the assignment variable in an RD setting. These tests are based on the key underpinnings of a valid RD design in that treated firms should not be able to precisely control the assignment variable around the deterministic cutoff. Thus, the results should show that the marginal density inSOP voting supportis continuous for our sample around the 70% cutoff.

In panel A ofTable 4, we test for discontinuities inSOP voting supportaround the 70% threshold for different bandwidths (±2.5%, ±5.0%,±10%) over the full sample period. We also examine the data-driven bandwidths developed by CJM for the full sample period and for the first year of SOP voting in 2011. We report the robust bias-corrected manipulation test statistic,p-value, and the number of treated and control firms in each range. A statistically significant manipulation test statistic would suggest that managers can successfully influence the assignment variable (SOP voting support) around the 70% threshold, while a nonsignificant statistic would indicate no detected manipulation. These results are used to determine whether the assignment of ISS treatment is random.

Our third validation exercise tests for differences in many preassignment characteristics between ISS-treated and control firms before the SOP vote.Table 4shows the average values for these characteristics for firms within a range of 2.5% above or below the 70% SOP voting support threshold. Columns 1 and 2 show the mean values for ISS-treated and control firms, respectively. The variables in this validation exercise can be grouped into 7 categories: SOP voting support, shareholder engagement, firm characteristics, CEO pay, director voting, governance, and board characteristics.Appendix Cdescribes all the variables. We compare certain director characteristics, such as the presence of a classified board and how many directors are voted on for the yeart+ 1, as they are known in advance and could potentially influence engagement. We compare most other variables in yeartjust before the low SOP vote. For many variables, we also examine the yeart—1 values (e.g., SOP voting support, engagement, and director voting) to ensure there are no trends that span for more than 1 year.

While most of the preassignment variables are commonly used in governance literature, we also consider whether unexpectedly high CEO pay might affect engagement.Expected CEO payis the estimate of the CEO’s total compensation using the regression methodology inRouen (2020).Unexpected CEO payis calculated as actual CEO total compensation lessexpected CEO pay. The results in panel B ofTable 4indicate that there are no significant univariate differences between firms just below or above the 70% threshold, except for the assignment variable (SOP voting support).

We also use local linear RD estimates to test for differences in preassignment variables, following the robust bias-corrected method proposed byCalonico et al. (2017), whose work we will describe further in the next subsection. The RD estimates andp-value, shown in columns 5 and 6, indicate that there are practically no significant differences between firms just below and just above the 70% threshold for most dimensions. The RD estimate forstock return volatilityis slightly negative and marginally significant at the 10% level, suggesting that ISS-treated firms have lower return volatility. But earlier analysis inTable 3shows that return volatility is not related to engagement in the overall universe of firms in the ISS database, so this difference is not expected to bias the results. We find that differences in preassignmentCC tenureare marginally significant at the 10% level in the RD test. However, further examination reported inFigure IA-1 of the Internet Appendixshows that this result is driven by outliers on each side of the 70% threshold.

Taken together, the results inTable 4andFigure 4support the notion that the distribution of firms around the 70% threshold for ISS treatment is largely random. We find no evidence that firms manipulate their SOP voting support to avoid treatment and the attributes of firms around the threshold are similar, which allows us to link changes in shareholder engagement to the effect of ISS’s policy.

4.2 Research design

To test the effect of ISS treatment on engagement, we consider the SOP vote by shareholders of firmiat timet, the annual meeting date. The percentage of votes approving SOP is the vote sharevit. Ifvitis below the 70% vote threshold,v∗⁠, then it receives ISS treatment, and we code the indicator for treatment asISS treatmentit=1⁠. Whenvit≥v∗⁠,ISS treatmentittakes the value of 0.

We estimate the effect of ISS treatment on the outcome variables using both an average treatment effect (ATE) estimation and an RD approach. The ATE estimation tests use this equation: y i t + 1 = κ + θ ISS treatmen t i t + X i t + φ j + μ t + ε i t , (2) where the coefficient of interest θ is the effect of ISS on the outcome variable y i t + 1 (e.g., engagement indicator ) after a low SOP vote. We include industry FEs ( ⁠ φ j ⁠ ) using one-digit SIC codes, which controls for time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity across industries, and fiscal year FEs ( μ t ) to control for time trends that affect all firms. X it is a vector of firm-level control variables identified as determinants of engagement in Table 3 , including size, market-to-book, return-on-assets, leverage, abnormal returns, ISS “against” SOP, ln(analysts), institutional ownership, classified boards, dual class shares, compensation committee tenure , and director voting support .

These tests are based on two key assumptions. The first is that, without the influence of ISS, the engagement of treated and control firms would have followed similar patterns (ie, the parallel trends assumption). We examine this assumption in Section 5.5.

The second assumption is the stable unit treatment value assumption (SUTVA). SUTVA holds that for an estimation to provide unbiased estimates of the average treatment effect, the impact on treated firms should not influence the outcomes of control firms (e.g.,Berg, Reisinger, and Streitz 2021). In our setting, control firms might learn from the response of ISS-treated firms, potentially reducing the cost of initiating their own engagement activities. These firms might also share similar compensation consultants or board members, a situation that could lead to knowledge spillovers on how to respond to a low SOP vote. Both situations, however, would lead us tounderestimatethe ATE of ISS’s policy on engagement by increasing the level of engagement of the control firms.

We also use an RD analysis to study the effect of ISS on the primary engagement measures:engagement indicatorandengagement count. We use a Sharp RD estimate that does not control for covariates or FEs becauseTable 4shows almost no significant differences in just under 50 preassignment covariates in treatment and control firms, andFigure 4andTable 4show no discontinuities in the assignment variable around the 70% threshold. We employ the robust RD method ofCalonico et al. (2017)that adjusts the confidence intervals and variance estimator to improve the accuracy of the coefficient estimates and statistical significance. For all tests, we report robust bias-corrected RD estimates.

To ensure the robustness of our RD specification, we test ISS treatment using different bandwidths around the 70% threshold (2.5%, 5.0%, and 10.0%) and the data-driven bandwidth selection method ofCalonico, Cattaneo, and Farrell (2020)(“CCF”). We primarily use a local linear RD estimate, as higher order polynomials can introduce bias (Gelman and Imbens 2019), but also report a local quadratic result for robustness. We use a triangular kernel to construct all local-polynomial estimators.

4.3 Engagement in the year after ISS treatment

This subsection examines how ISS affects shareholder engagement in the year following treatment. We first provide summary statistics and then conduct the regression analysis to test H1 using a subset of firms whose SOP votes fall within ±2.5% of 70% support. We also use varying bandwidths around the 70% threshold for the RD analysis.

Panel A ofTable 5shows the average, median, and standard deviation of engagement measures for ISS treated and control firms that had SOP votes within ±2.5% of the 70% support threshold. In the year after receiving a low SOP vote, 58.5% of the sample firms report shareholder engagement, on average using 2.1 engagement words. ISS treated firms are 14.9-percentage-points more likely to report shareholder engagement and use 0.74 more engagement words than control firms, both of which are significant at the 1% level or better using two-tailedt-tests. These differences are economically large and reflect a 25% and 36% increase, respectively, versus the mean values for treated and control firms.

Approximately 52% of the sample firms report engaging with investors in response to the previous year’s low SOP vote. About 14% provide a table detailing shareholders’ concerns and the board’s response. On average, sample firms contact shareholders owning 17% of shares and report speaking with owners of 12% of outstanding shares. When only considering firms that disclose engagement, they contact shareholders holding an average of 58% of shares and spoke with holders of 42% of shares. ISS treated firms engage with shareholders holding an average of 13.4% more shares (23.7% vs. 10.3%) and spoke with investors holding 9.4% more shares (17.2% vs. 7.8%) compared to control firms. Both differences are significant at the 1% level and reflect an 81% and 76% increase versus the sample mean, respectively.

In panel B, we study the relation between ISS treatment and engagement. We estimateEquation (2)using OLS regressions for most of the dependent variables and a Poisson regression for theengagement count. The results show thatISS treatmentis positively related to the presence and intensity of shareholder engagement in the following year. In all six regressions, the coefficient forISS treatmentis statistically significant at the 1% level and the size of the engagement differences is substantial. For example, treated firms are 16.0-percentage-points more likely to report engagement (column 1), which is a 27% increase from the sample mean of 58.5%. The coefficient forISS treatmentin column 2 is 0.407, indicating that theengagement countfor treated firms is 50% higher.18In columns 3 to 6, we find similar results showing a positive and significant relation between ISS treatment and various measures of the depth of shareholder engagement.

Table 6reports the RD analysis of engagement. It includes the RD estimate,p-values, number of firms in the ISS treated and control sample for each bandwidth, and polynomial type. Panel A presents the results for theengagement indicatoras the dependent variable, showing that ISS treatment leads to a significantly higher probability of engagement in all five RD specifications.

4.4 Shareholder engagement and ISS sanctions

4.4.1 Ex ante director voting support

In this subsection, we test whether firms whose board members have greater incentives to avoid a negative recommendation from ISS are more responsive to ISS treatment. H2a posits that boards with a greater threat of ISS sanctions, such as those with directors who had previously received lower shareholder voting support, will demonstrate a stronger engagement response to avoid ISS opposition to their reelection.

To test this, we modifyEquation (2)to include controls for ex antecompensation committee (CC) member voting supportanddirector voting support, which reflect the previous year’s voting support levels. We also include the interactions between these two voting support metrics andISS treatment. The dependent variables areengagement indicatorandengagement count. The variable of interest in each regression isISS treatment×voting support. If firms facing a greater risk of sanctions under ISS treatment intensify engagement efforts more, we expect to see a negative coefficient for this interaction term.

InTable 7, the coefficient forvoting supportis positive and significant at either the 5% or 10% level in all regressions, indicating a relation between ex ante voting support and engagement. For tests of the interaction term,ISS treatment×voting support, we find no evidence that ISS treatment influences the extensive margin of engagement (columns 1 and 2). However, in testing for the intensive margin of engagement in columns 3 and 4, we find significant negative coefficients for the interaction terms for both director-voting based measures. This evidence suggests that while directors who are more susceptible to ISS sanctions (ie, lower prior voting support) are not significantly more likely to engage, they do so more intensively, likely to demonstrate a sufficiently robust response in accordance with ISS’s policies. Thus, our findings offer support for H2a, indicating that the threat of ISS opposing their reelection prompts directors to adhere to ISS’s engagement policies in response to a low SOP vote.

4.4.2 Ex post ISS recommendations

Our hypotheses and inferences above rely on (1) the credibility of ISS’s threat of recommending against the directors of nonresponsive treated firms, and(2)directors being responsive to avoid the negative recommendations. In this subsection, we formally test H2b, namely, that ISS will follow through with the threat to recommend voting against the directors of less responsive firms.

Because we do not have access to ISS’s proprietary evaluation of firm responsiveness, we classify firms as more or less responsive based on whether they disclose engagement in their subsequent proxy statements. Our dependent variables in these analyses are whether ISS recommends voting against compensation committee members and separately members of the full board after a low SOP vote. We again compare ISS-treated and control firms, categorized by whether they engage with shareholders. If the ISS threat is credible, we would expect less responsive treated firms to be more likely to receive “against” recommendations for their directors than more responsive treated firms. We also posit that ISS’s increased scrutiny will result in more negative voting recommendations for treated than control firms within the same category of responsiveness.

In our test, we designate firms asmore responsiveif they disclose engagement in yeart+ 1 andless responsiveif not. We then evaluate the likelihood that ISS recommends voting against board members in yeart+ 1. If ISS’s threat is credible, we anticipate ISS would be more likely to recommend voting “against” directors of less responsive firms, especially those on the compensation committee. Additionally, because of increased scrutiny on treated firms, we expect that ISS would be more likely to recommend voting “against” directors of treated firms than directors of control firms with the same responsiveness.

Panel A ofTable 8reveals that while ISS recommends voting against 23.9% of treated directors on the compensation committee, this increases to 42.9% for less responsive firms, compared to 13.3% of more responsive firms. This 29.5-percentage-point difference (significant at the 1% level) is consistent with the threat of a negative ISS voting recommendation being credible for firms whose votes fall below the 70% threshold. In contrast, compensation committee members for less responsive control firms see an insignificant change in ISS recommendations. Further, column 2 shows that ISS more heavily penalizes treated firms that are less responsive, with a 31.4-percentage-point greater chance of an “against” recommendation for their compensation committee members compared to control firms.

4.5 Engagement over time after ISS treatment

Next, we examine the impact of ISS treatment on engagement over time. These tests have two goals. First, we want to assess the parallel trends assumption. Second, we wish to determine whether the initial increase in engagement after ISS treatment persists (H3). To do this, we measure engagement over a 9-year period centered on the low SOP vote and use indicator variables in an event-time d-i-d design to estimate dynamic treatment effects.

To formally test the impact of ISS on shareholder engagement over time, we first use a panel regression to estimate this equation: Engagemen t i t + 1 = κ + θ ISS treatmen t i t + X i t + ω i + μ t + ε i t , (3) where the coefficient of interest θ is the effect of ISS on shareholder engagement. We test the presence ( engagement indicator ) and intensity ( engagement count ) of engagement separately using a FE OLS and Poisson estimation, respectively. We include firm FEs ( ⁠ ω i ) and fiscal year FEs ( μ t ) to control for time trends affecting all firms. The vector of firm controls, X it , is identical to Equation (2) but varies over time.

Second, to determine whether the pre- and post-ISS treatment results in engagement variation, we also estimateEquation (3)after replacing the ISS indicator with six indicator variables (ISS– 1,ISS0,ISS+1⁠,ISS+2⁠,ISS+3⁠, andISS+4)that indicate the year relative to the ISS treatment with a low SOP vote. The values for years –4, –3, and –2 are included in the intercept to serve as the baseline comparison. The coefficients forISS– 1andISS0are important because they reveal any differences in engagement between treatment and control groups before the low SOP vote, which formally attempts to falsify the parallel trends assumption.19

The coefficient forISS+1is important because it shows how quickly firms change their shareholder engagement activities in response to ISS treatment. ISS considers the timing of shareholder engagement in its evaluation of a sufficiently robust response. The coefficients forISS+2⁠,ISS+3⁠, andISS+4are also important because they capture longer-term differences in engagement. If firms continue to engage at a higher rate in future years, some of these coefficients should be positive and statistically significant. These coefficients test H3, which posits that engagement after ISS treatment will persist over time for the reasons detailed in Subsection 2.3.

We first estimate the effect of ISS treatment on shareholder engagement with only FEs. Column 1 ofTable 9shows that ISS treatment significantly increases shareholder engagement, as indicated by a coefficient of 0.214 and at-statistic of 6.24 (p< 0.001) in tests ofengagement indicator. The effect is also economically significant, with a 47% increase in shareholder engagement compared to the full-panel average (0.36). The results are similar usingengagement countas the dependent variable.

4.6 Event study

Next, we use an event study to analyze the shareholder response to ISS treatment. While we recognize possible arguments for why there might be a positive, neutral, or negative shareholder response (H4a), we hypothesize the market response to ISS treatment will be positive for firms that have already received an “against” recommendation from ISS (H4b).

To test H4a and H4b, we analyze abnormal stock returns using the following framework. First, we calculate 3-day and 5-day firm-level compounded returns for the period centered on the date the firm first released the 8-K filing containing its SOP voting results. 20 Abnormal returns are calculated as the difference between the firm and CRSP value-weighted index returns during the same period. 21 Using abnormal returns , we estimate OLS regressions to test the relation between ISS treatment, ISS recommendations, and shareholder returns using the following equation for H4a: A bnormalreturn s i t = α + β 1 I S S treatmen t i t + β 2 I S S ‘ ‘ against ″ S O P i t + β 3 SOP voting suppor t i t + φ j + μ t + ε i t , (4) and the following equation for H4b: A bnormal return s i t = α + β 1 I S S treatmen t i t × ISS ‘ ‘ against ″ S O P i t + β 2 ISS treatmen t i t + β 3 ISS ‘ ‘ against ″ S O P i t + β 4 S O P voting suppor t i t + φ j + μ t + ε i t , (5) where ISS treatment is set to one for firms that received between 67.50% and 69.99% SOP voting support and zero for firms that received 70.00% to 72.50%. The variable ISS “against” SOP is set to one if ISS recommended voting against the SOP proposal. SOP voting support is the disclosed percentage of shareholder votes that supported the SOP proposal. By examining a narrow bandwidth around 70% and controlling for SOP voting support as well as the ISS treatment effect, this specification is functionally similar to a regression discontinuity design. We include industry FEs ( ⁠ φ j ⁠ ) using one-digit SIC codes to control for time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity across industries, and fiscal year FEs ( μ t ) to control for time trends that affect all firms.
